content.problemStatement:
You are given the sizes of angles of a simple quadrilateral (in degrees) AAA, BBB, CCC and DDD, in some order along its perimeter. Determine whether the quadrilateral is cyclic.

Note: A quadrilateral is cyclic if and only if the sum of opposite angles is 180∘180^{\circ}180∘.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains four space‑separated integers A, B, C and D — the interior angles of a quadrilateral in order along its perimeter.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the quadrilateral is cyclic, otherwise "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Case 1: A+C=10+30=40≠180, so NOT cyclic.\nCase 2: A+C=10+170=180 and B+D=20+160=180, both conditions hold → cyclic.\nCase 3: B+D=1+1=2≠180, so NOT cyclic.
First quadrilateral: A+C=90+90=180 and B+D=90+90=180 → YES.\nSecond quadrilateral: A+C=120+120=240≠180 → NO.
A+C=100+80=180 and B+D=80+100=180, therefore the quadrilateral is cyclic.

solution.approach: A simple quadrilateral is cyclic iff each pair of opposite angles sums to 180°. Because the angles are given in order, the opposite pairs are (A, C) and (B, D). For each test case we check whether A + C == 180 and B + D == 180. If both conditions hold we output YES, otherwise NO. The algorithm runs in O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
